behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: The core failure here is incorrect grouping logic—entries with identical trace IDs end up in different groups. This is an in-process algorithm that operates on data structures (dictionaries, lists). A pure in-process mock doesn't hide the failure: we can construct test data representing entries from different source services and verify they group correctly. No I/O, OS, or subprocess behavior is required.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5 log entries: 3 from service-A and 2 from service-B, all sharing trace ID 'tx-123'. Parse them in any order, then verify the grouping function returns exactly one group containing all 5 entries, with correct source service labels preserved. Repeat with different trace IDs to ensure no cross-contamination.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test operates on in-memory data structures; the grouping algorithm is the unit under test, not I/O or concurrency.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: The failure is incorrect ordering of entries when timestamps are slightly out of sync. The sort logic itself is a pure algorithm operating on timestamp values. A mock doesn't hide this: we can pass test timestamps with known clock skew and verify the ordering. No real time, OS clock, or I/O is required—we just need to simulate the skew in the test data.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10 log entries with timestamps: service-A emits at t=1000, service-B emits at t=550 (500ms earlier). Place all in random input order. Verify the ordering function produces strictly sorted order by timestamp, with service-B entries appearing first despite being emitted from a different service. Verify the sort is stable and consistent across multiple runs.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test uses in-memory timestamps with simulated skew; ordering algorithm is deterministic and testable at Unit level.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: The failure is incorrect statistical detection—the anomaly detector misses spikes or triggers false positives. This is a pure algorithm: given latency samples, compute p99 and compare to baseline. We can inject test latencies with known distributions (e.g., 100 normal + 1 spike) and verify the detection logic. No real metrics collection, time passage, or I/O required.
behaviors.test_description: Provide 100 latency samples with baseline p99 = 200ms. Add 5 spike samples at 650ms (3.25x baseline). Run the anomaly detector and verify: (1) spike is flagged in the report, (2) spike threshold is calculated as ≥600ms (3x baseline), (3) non-spike samples are not flagged. Verify behavior at boundary: spike at exactly 600ms is flagged, spike at 599ms is not.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test provides synthetic latency arrays; statistical algorithm is pure and deterministic at Unit level.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: The failure is a crash or miscount when encountering malformed JSON. The error handling logic is testable in isolation: pass a malformed JSON string to the parser, verify it catches the exception and returns a skip signal (or counts it), and the parser doesn't raise an unhandled exception. Mocking the I/O layer doesn't hide this—the core logic is the error handler, which we can test directly.
behaviors.test_description: Pass a sequence of 10 log entries to the parser: 7 valid JSON + 3 malformed (missing quotes, truncated, invalid escapes). Verify: (1) parser does not crash, (2) 7 valid entries are returned, (3) skip counter = 3, (4) error messages or logs for skipped entries are produced. Repeat with 100% malformed input to verify robustness.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test directly invokes error handling logic with test data; pipeline crash behavior is deterministic and local to the parser.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: The failure is real I/O bottleneck—entries are dropped, buffering overflows, or the tool can't keep up with real network or file I/O at high throughput. Mocking I/O (Unit or Integration in-memory tests) would bypass latency: a mock returns instantly, hiding real network timeouts, file descriptor limits, or kernel buffer exhaustion. The tool must ingest from multiple microservices (implies real HTTP I/O). Only System-level testing with actual I/O at 10k/sec rate catches whether the tool truly handles the load or degrades.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a load test that streams 10,000 JSON log entries per second into the tool from multiple real or simulated (but real-latency) sources (e.g., 5 HTTP endpoints each sending 2,000/sec) for 10 seconds total = 100,000 entries. Measure: (1) no entries dropped (output count = 100,000), (2) no buffering errors or crashes, (3) processing latency stays within SLA (e.g., 95th percentile < 2s end-to-end). Repeat under memory-constrained or slow-disk conditions to expose resource bottlenecks.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real I/O performance characteristics and system resource constraints (OS buffers, file descriptors, network timeouts); mocked or in-process I/O would not expose real failure modes.
